title: Propositional Logic - Section C Question Bank

questions.q: Verify: P ∧ (¬P ∨ Q') ≡ (P ⇒ Q)'

steps.action: Replace the prime notation (Q') with standard logical negation (¬Q).
steps.note: This makes all operators uniform for simplification.

steps.action: Apply the Distributive Law: P ∧ (X ∨ Y) = (P ∧ X) ∨ (P ∧ Y). Here X = ¬P, Y = ¬Q.
steps.note: This distributes P into the disjunction.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: P ∧ ¬P = False (a proposition cannot be both true and false).
steps.law: Complement law (also called Law of contradiction)
steps.note: The first term becomes logically false.

steps.action: Apply the Identity Law for disjunction: False ∨ X = X. Remove the false term.
steps.note: The expression now contains only P and ¬Q.

steps.action: Recall the definition of implication: P ⇒ Q ≡ ¬P ∨ Q. We want to relate P ∧ ¬Q to this.
steps.note: Because ¬(¬P ∨ Q) simplifies to P ∧ ¬Q by De Morgan.

steps.action: Apply De Morgan’s Law: ¬(¬P ∨ Q) = ¬¬P ∧ ¬Q.
steps.law: De Morgan: ¬(A ∨ B) = ¬A ∧ ¬B, then double negation ¬¬P = P
steps.note: This confirms that P ∧ ¬Q is exactly the negation of P ⇒ Q.

steps.action: Conclude that P ∧ ¬Q is the negation of (P ⇒ Q).
steps.note: The left side has been transformed to ¬(P ⇒ Q).

steps.action: Rewrite the negation using the prime notation as required by the right-hand side.
steps.note: This matches the target expression exactly.

questions.explanation: Starting from P ∧ (¬P ∨ Q'), we replace Q' with ¬Q, distribute, simplify the contradiction to False, then use identity to get P ∧ ¬Q. Recognizing that P ∧ ¬Q is ¬(P ⇒ Q) and converting back to prime notation proves the equivalence.

questions.q: Show that [(p ⇒ q) ∧ (q ⇒ r)] ⇒ (p ⇒ r) is a tautology.
questions.answer: The statement is a tautology (Hypothetical Syllogism).

steps.action: Rewrite each implication using the law p ⇒ q ≡ ¬p ∨ q.
steps.note: This eliminates all → symbols, leaving only ∨, ∧, and ¬.

steps.action: Apply the implication law to the outer ⇒: A ⇒ B ≡ ¬A ∨ B. Here A = (¬p ∨ q) ∧ (¬q ∨ r), B = (¬p ∨ r).
steps.note: The whole statement becomes a disjunction: negation of the antecedent OR the consequent.

steps.action: Apply De Morgan's law to the negation of the conjunction: ¬(X ∧ Y) ≡ ¬X ∨ ¬Y.
steps.note: The negation distributes over the ∧, turning it into ∨ of negations.

steps.action: Apply De Morgan's law again to each inner negation: ¬(¬p ∨ q) ≡ ¬¬p ∧ ¬q, and ¬(¬q ∨ r) ≡ ¬¬q ∧ ¬r.
steps.note: Now we have negations of disjunctions becoming conjunctions of negations.

steps.action: Apply double negation elimination: ¬¬p ≡ p, ¬¬q ≡ q.
steps.note: The double negations cancel out, leaving simple literals.

steps.action: Use associativity and commutativity of ∨ to rearrange terms: group (p ∧ ¬q) with ¬p, and (q ∧ ¬r) with r.
steps.note: This regrouping helps to apply distributive laws separately.

steps.action: Simplify (p ∧ ¬q) ∨ ¬p. Apply the distributive law in reverse: (p ∧ ¬q) ∨ ¬p ≡ (¬p ∨ p) ∧ (¬p ∨ ¬q).
steps.law: Distributivity, complement law (¬p ∨ p = True), identity law
steps.note: Alternatively, notice that if ¬p is true, the whole is true; if ¬p is false (p true), then (p∧¬q) forces ¬q. This simplifies to ¬p ∨ ¬q.

steps.action: Simplify (q ∧ ¬r) ∨ r similarly: (q ∧ ¬r) ∨ r ≡ (r ∨ q) ∧ (r ∨ ¬r) ≡ (r ∨ q) ∧ True ≡ r ∨ q.
steps.law: Distributivity, complement law (r ∨ ¬r = True), identity law
steps.note: This is symmetric to step 7; the result is r ∨ q.

steps.action: Now the whole expression becomes (¬p ∨ ¬q) ∨ (r ∨ q). Remove parentheses (associative law).
steps.note: We have a disjunction of four literals.

steps.action: Group ¬q and q together: ¬p ∨ (¬q ∨ q) ∨ r.
steps.note: ¬q and q cancel each other, leaving True.

steps.action: Apply the domination law: True ∨ X = True.
steps.note: The entire expression simplifies to the constant True.

steps.action: Conclude that the original statement is a tautology.
steps.note: Since the expression simplifies to True for all truth assignments, it is a tautology.

questions.explanation: This is the hypothetical syllogism: if p implies q and q implies r, then p implies r. Using logical equivalences (implication laws, De Morgan, distribution, complement, and identity), the whole expression reduces to True, proving it is a tautology.

questions.q: Show that [(p ⇒ q) ∧ p] ⇒ q is a tautology.
questions.answer: The statement is a tautology (Modus Ponens).

steps.action: Rewrite the implication p ⇒ q as ¬p ∨ q.
steps.note: This eliminates the implication symbol inside the antecedent.

steps.action: Apply the Distributive Law to the antecedent: (¬p ∨ q) ∧ p = (¬p ∧ p) ∨ (q ∧ p).
steps.note: p is distributed over the disjunction.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: ¬p ∧ p = False.
steps.note: A statement and its negation cannot both be true.

steps.action: Apply the Identity Law for disjunction: False ∨ X = X.
steps.note: The antecedent simplifies to p ∧ q.

steps.action: Now the whole statement becomes (p ∧ q) ⇒ q. Rewrite this implication as ¬(p ∧ q) ∨ q.
steps.note: Convert back to disjunction form.

steps.action: Apply De Morgan’s Law: ¬(p ∧ q) = ¬p ∨ ¬q.
steps.note: Negation of a conjunction becomes a disjunction of negations.

steps.action: Use associativity to regroup: ¬p ∨ (¬q ∨ q).
steps.note: Group the terms involving q.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: ¬q ∨ q = True.
steps.note: q is either true or false.

steps.action: Apply the Domination Law: ¬p ∨ True = True.
steps.note: The whole expression is always true.

questions.explanation: This is Modus Ponens: if p implies q and p is true, then q must be true. The algebraic simplification shows the whole implication reduces to True.

questions.q: Verify: P ∨ (¬P ∧ Q) ≡ P ∨ Q

steps.action: Apply the Distributive Law in reverse: P ∨ (X ∧ Y) = (P ∨ X) ∧ (P ∨ Y). Here X = ¬P, Y = Q.
steps.note: This is the reverse of the usual distribution.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: P ∨ ¬P = True (law of excluded middle).
steps.note: A proposition or its negation is always true.

steps.action: Apply the Identity Law for conjunction: True ∧ X = X.
steps.note: The True term disappears, leaving P ∨ Q.

questions.explanation: This is a form of the absorption law. Intuitively, if P is true, both sides are true; if P is false, the left side becomes Q and the right side becomes Q. Hence they are equivalent.

questions.q: Simplify: (P ∧ Q) ∨ (P ∧ ¬Q) ∨ (¬P ∧ Q)

steps.action: Group the first two terms: (P ∧ Q) ∨ (P ∧ ¬Q). Factor out P using the distributive law.
steps.note: P is common in the first two conjuncts.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: Q ∨ ¬Q = True.
steps.note: Q is either true or false, so Q∨¬Q is always true.

steps.action: Apply the Identity Law: P ∧ True = P.
steps.note: The expression reduces to P ∨ (¬P ∧ Q).

steps.action: Now apply the distributive law again: P ∨ (¬P ∧ Q) = (P ∨ ¬P) ∧ (P ∨ Q).
steps.note: This is the same pattern as in the previous question.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: P ∨ ¬P = True.
steps.note: The first disjunction is always true.

steps.action: Apply the Identity Law: True ∧ (P ∨ Q) = P ∨ Q.
steps.note: The final simplified form is P ∨ Q.

questions.explanation: The expression covers all cases where at least one of P or Q is true, except the case where both are false. Thus it simplifies to P ∨ Q.

questions.q: Prove that (P → Q) ∨ (Q → P) is a tautology.

steps.action: Rewrite each implication using p → q ≡ ¬p ∨ q.
steps.note: This eliminates the → symbols.

steps.action: Remove parentheses using the associative law (since ∨ is associative).
steps.note: The order of evaluation does not matter.

steps.action: Rearrange the terms using the commutative law to group complementary pairs together.
steps.note: Group ¬P with P and Q with ¬Q.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: ¬P ∨ P = True and Q ∨ ¬Q = True.
steps.note: Each complementary pair is always true.

steps.action: Apply the Idempotent law (or Domination): True ∨ True = True.
steps.note: Disjunction of True with anything is True.

questions.explanation: For any P and Q, either P→Q or Q→P must be true. This is a classic tautology that follows from the law of excluded middle.

questions.q: Using truth table, check if (P ⊕ Q) and (P ↔ ¬Q) are logically equivalent.

steps.action: List all possible combinations of truth values for P and Q (4 rows).
steps.note: Each row represents a possible scenario.

steps.action: Compute P ⊕ Q (exclusive OR): true when P and Q differ.
steps.note: XOR is true iff exactly one operand is true.

steps.action: Compute ¬Q (negation of Q).
steps.note: Flip the truth value of Q.

steps.action: Compute P ↔ ¬Q (biconditional): true when P and ¬Q have the same value.
steps.note: Biconditional is true iff both sides are equal.

steps.action: Compare the column for P⊕Q with the column for P↔¬Q.
steps.note: All corresponding entries match.

questions.explanation: The truth tables for (P⊕Q) and (P↔¬Q) are identical; therefore the two expressions are logically equivalent. XOR is true exactly when P and Q differ, which is the same as P being equivalent to ¬Q.

questions.q: Simplify: (P → Q) ∧ (¬P → Q)

steps.action: Rewrite both implications using p → q ≡ ¬p ∨ q.
steps.note: First implication: P→Q = ¬P∨Q. Second: ¬P→Q = ¬(¬P)∨Q.

steps.action: Apply double negation: ¬¬P = P.
steps.note: This simplifies the second clause.

steps.action: Use the distributive law in reverse: (X ∨ Q) ∧ (Y ∨ Q) = Q ∨ (X ∧ Y). Here X = ¬P, Y = P.
steps.note: Factor out Q from both clauses.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: ¬P ∧ P = False.
steps.note: A statement and its negation cannot both be true.

steps.action: Apply the Identity Law for disjunction: Q ∨ False = Q.
steps.note: The false term disappears.

questions.explanation: Whether P is true or false, both implications force Q to be true. Therefore the conjunction simplifies to just Q.

questions.q: Prove the resolution rule: ((P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ R)) → (Q ∨ R) is a tautology.

steps.action: Assume the antecedent is true: (P ∨ Q) = True and (¬P ∨ R) = True. We aim to show (Q ∨ R) must be True.
steps.law: Implication is false only if antecedent true and consequent false
steps.note: We search for a counterexample; if none exists, it's a tautology.

steps.action: Consider the two possible truth values of P (law of excluded middle).
steps.expression: Case 1: P = True. Case 2: P = False.
steps.note: Every scenario falls into one of these two cases.

steps.action: Case 1: P = True. Then from (¬P ∨ R) we have (False ∨ R) = R, so R must be True. Hence Q ∨ R is True (since R is True).
steps.note: If P is true, the second clause forces R true.

steps.action: Case 2: P = False. Then from (P ∨ Q) we have (False ∨ Q) = Q, so Q must be True. Hence Q ∨ R is True (since Q is True).
steps.note: If P is false, the first clause forces Q true.

steps.action: In both cases, whenever the antecedent holds, the consequent (Q ∨ R) holds. No assignment makes antecedent true and consequent false.
steps.note: The implication cannot be false.

steps.action: Therefore ((P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ R)) → (Q ∨ R) is a tautology.
steps.note: The formula is true for all truth assignments.

questions.explanation: The resolution rule is fundamental in logic programming and automated theorem proving. This proof shows that if both clauses (P∨Q) and (¬P∨R) are satisfied, then (Q∨R) must be satisfied.

questions.q: Show that (P ∧ (P → Q)) → Q is a tautology using a truth table.

steps.action: List all combinations of P and Q (4 rows).
steps.note: We evaluate the formula for each row.

steps.action: Compute P → Q for each row: false only when P=1 and Q=0.
steps.note: Implication is true except when antecedent true and consequent false.

steps.action: Compute P ∧ (P → Q): true only when both P and (P→Q) are true.
steps.note: Conjunction requires both operands true.

steps.action: Compute the final implication [P ∧ (P→Q)] → Q: false only when antecedent true and consequent false.
steps.note: In row4, antecedent=1 and consequent=1, so true; all others have antecedent=0, so true.

steps.action: Observe that the final column is all 1s (True).
steps.note: The formula holds in every possible situation.

questions.explanation: The truth table shows that (P ∧ (P→Q)) → Q is true for all four assignments, hence a tautology (Modus Ponens).

questions.q: Simplify: (P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ Q) ∧ (P ∨ ¬Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ ¬Q)

steps.action: Combine the first two clauses: (P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ Q). As shown in Q8, this simplifies to Q.
steps.note: Because if Q is true, both clauses are true; if Q is false, one of them becomes false.

steps.action: Combine the last two clauses: (P ∨ ¬Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ ¬Q). Using the same pattern with ¬Q in place of Q, this simplifies to ¬Q.
steps.law: Same as step 1: (X∨Y)∧(¬X∨Y) ≡ Y, here Y = ¬Q
steps.note: Replace Q by ¬Q in the pattern.

steps.action: Now the entire conjunction reduces to Q ∧ ¬Q.
steps.law: Conjunction of the two simplified parts
steps.note: We have combined all four original clauses into two.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: Q ∧ ¬Q = False.
steps.note: A proposition cannot be both true and false.

questions.explanation: The four clauses together force both Q and ¬Q to be true, which is impossible. Therefore the entire expression is a contradiction (False).

questions.q: Verify the equivalence: (P → (Q → R)) ≡ ((P ∧ Q) → R)

steps.action: Start with the left side: P → (Q → R). Rewrite the inner implication Q → R as ¬Q ∨ R.
steps.note: Remove the inner → first.

steps.action: Rewrite the outer implication P → (¬Q ∨ R) as ¬P ∨ (¬Q ∨ R).
steps.note: Now both → have been eliminated.

steps.action: Use the associative law to group ¬P and ¬Q together: (¬P ∨ ¬Q) ∨ R.
steps.note: Parentheses do not matter for ∨.

steps.action: Apply De Morgan's law to ¬P ∨ ¬Q, which is equivalent to ¬(P ∧ Q).
steps.note: The disjunction of negations is the negation of the conjunction.

steps.action: Rewrite the disjunction ¬(P ∧ Q) ∨ R as an implication: (P ∧ Q) → R.
steps.note: This matches the right side exactly.

questions.explanation: This is the law of exportation, which shows that a nested implication is equivalent to an implication with a conjunctive antecedent.

questions.q: Construct a truth table for (P ∨ ¬Q) → (Q ∧ ¬P). Is it a tautology, contradiction, or contingency?

steps.action: List all 4 combinations of P and Q.
steps.note: Each row represents a possible scenario.

steps.action: Compute ¬Q and P ∨ ¬Q for each row.
steps.note: P∨¬Q is false only when P=0 and ¬Q=0 (i.e., P=0, Q=1).

steps.action: Compute ¬P and Q ∧ ¬P for each row.
steps.note: Q∧¬P is true only when Q=1 and P=0.

steps.action: Compute the implication (P∨¬Q) → (Q∧¬P). Recall: A→B is false only when A=true and B=false.
steps.note: Only row2 gives true; others false.

steps.action: Determine the type: not all true (so not tautology), not all false (so not contradiction).
steps.note: A contingency has at least one true and one false.

questions.conclusion: Contingency (not all true, not all false)
questions.explanation: Only the assignment P=0, Q=1 makes the implication true; all other assignments make it false. Hence it is a contingency.

questions.q: Prove that (P ↔ Q) ≡ (P → Q) ∧ (Q → P) using logical laws.

steps.action: Recall the definition of the biconditional (if and only if).
steps.expression: P ↔ Q is defined as (P → Q) ∧ (Q → P)
steps.note: This is the standard logical equivalence used in logic.

questions.explanation: By definition, the biconditional is true exactly when both implications hold. Therefore the equivalence is immediate and requires no further steps.

questions.q: Simplify: (P → Q) ∨ (P → ¬Q)

steps.action: Rewrite each implication using p → q ≡ ¬p ∨ q.
steps.note: This eliminates the → symbols.

steps.action: Use the associative law to remove parentheses (since ∨ is associative).
steps.note: The order of evaluation does not matter.

steps.action: Apply the idempotent law: ¬P ∨ ¬P = ¬P.
steps.note: Duplicate ¬P is reduced to a single occurrence.

steps.action: Group Q and ¬Q together: ¬P ∨ (Q ∨ ¬Q).
steps.note: Parentheses are for clarity.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: Q ∨ ¬Q = True.
steps.note: Q is either true or false.

steps.action: Apply the Domination Law: ¬P ∨ True = True.
steps.note: The whole expression is always true.

questions.explanation: For any P, either P→Q or P→¬Q must be true (if P is false both are true; if P is true, then either Q or ¬Q is true). Hence the disjunction is a tautology.

questions.q: Check validity: If it rains then the ground is wet. The ground is not wet. Therefore, it did not rain. Write in propositional form and verify using Modus Tollens.
questions.propositions: R: It rains, W: Ground is wet

steps.action: Translate the English statements into propositional logic: 'If it rains then the ground is wet' becomes R → W. 'The ground is not wet' becomes ¬W. The conclusion 'it did not rain' is ¬R.
steps.note: We now have a logical argument.

steps.action: Recall Modus Tollens: From P → Q and ¬Q, we can infer ¬P.
steps.note: This is a valid inference rule.

steps.action: Substitute P = R, Q = W. Then the premises become R → W and ¬W.
steps.expression: Applying Modus Tollens with P=R, Q=W gives ¬R
steps.note: The pattern matches exactly.

steps.action: Therefore the conclusion ¬R follows logically from the premises.
steps.note: No counterexample exists.

questions.explanation: The argument is a classic example of Modus Tollens: if the implication and the negation of the consequent hold, the negation of the antecedent must hold. Hence it is valid.

questions.q: Prove that (P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ Q) ∧ (P ∨ ¬Q) is equivalent to P ∧ Q.

steps.action: First, combine the first two clauses: (P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ Q). As previously shown, this simplifies to Q.
steps.note: This uses the fact that Q must be true for both clauses to hold.

steps.action: Now the whole expression becomes Q ∧ (P ∨ ¬Q).
steps.note: We replaced the first two clauses with Q.

steps.action: Apply the Distributive Law: Q ∧ (P ∨ ¬Q) = (Q ∧ P) ∨ (Q ∧ ¬Q).
steps.note: Distribute Q over the disjunction inside.

steps.action: Apply the Complement Law: Q ∧ ¬Q = False.
steps.note: A proposition cannot be both true and false.

steps.action: Apply the Identity Law for disjunction: (Q ∧ P) ∨ False = Q ∧ P.
steps.law: Identity law (X ∨ False = X), and commutativity
steps.note: The false term disappears.

questions.explanation: The three clauses force both P and Q to be true. The expression simplifies to P ∧ Q.

questions.q: Use logical equivalences to show that ¬(P → Q) ≡ P ∧ ¬Q.

steps.action: Rewrite the implication inside the negation: P → Q ≡ ¬P ∨ Q.
steps.note: Replace → with its definition.

steps.action: Apply De Morgan's Law to the negation of a disjunction: ¬(¬P ∨ Q) = ¬¬P ∧ ¬Q.
steps.note: The negation distributes and flips the ∨ to ∧.

steps.action: Apply Double Negation: ¬¬P = P.
steps.note: Two negations cancel out.

questions.explanation: This is a standard equivalence: the negation of an implication is a conjunction of the antecedent and the negation of the consequent.

questions.q: Prove that the following argument is invalid by finding a counterexample: If I study then I will pass. I did not study. Therefore, I will not pass.
questions.propositions: S: I study, P: I pass
questions.counterexample: S = false, P = true. Then S→P is true (false→true), ¬S is true, but ¬P is false. So premises true, conclusion false.

steps.action: Translate into propositional form: Premises = S → P, ¬S; Conclusion = ¬P.
steps.note: We check if the conclusion logically follows.

steps.action: To prove invalidity, find an assignment where all premises are true but the conclusion is false.
steps.note: An argument is valid iff no such assignment exists.

steps.action: Try S = false. Then ¬S is true. The premise S→P becomes false→P, which is true regardless of P. So both premises are true.
steps.note: False → anything is true.

steps.action: Now choose P = true. Then the conclusion ¬P becomes false. So we have premises true and conclusion false.
steps.note: This yields a counterexample.

steps.action: Since a counterexample exists (S=false, P=true), the argument is invalid.
steps.note: The fallacy is called 'denying the antecedent'.

questions.explanation: The argument commits the fallacy of denying the antecedent. Even if you don't study, you could still pass by other means, so the conclusion does not follow.

questions.q: Simplify using Boolean algebra: (P → Q) ∧ (¬P → R) ∧ (Q → S) ∧ (R → S)

steps.action: Rewrite all implications: X → Y = ¬X ∨ Y.
steps.note: This puts everything into conjunctive normal form.

steps.action: Apply the resolution rule to (¬P ∨ Q) and (¬Q ∨ S). Resolution says (A ∨ B) ∧ (¬B ∨ C) ⇒ (A ∨ C). Here A = ¬P, B = Q, C = S. This yields (¬P ∨ S).
steps.note: We are deriving a new clause that is logically implied by the conjunction.

steps.action: Apply resolution to (P ∨ R) and (¬R ∨ S). Here A = P, B = R, C = S. This yields (P ∨ S).
steps.note: Again, a derived clause.

steps.action: Now we have (¬P ∨ S) ∧ (P ∨ S) as part of the simplified expression (the other clauses are implied by these).
steps.note: The original conjunction implies these two clauses.

steps.action: Simplify (¬P ∨ S) ∧ (P ∨ S) using the distributive law: (¬P ∧ P) ∨ S = False ∨ S = S.
steps.law: Distribution: (X∨Z)∧(Y∨Z) ≡ Z∨(X∧Y), then complement and identity
steps.note: This shows that S must be true.

questions.explanation: The four implications together force S to be true regardless of P. If P is true, then Q must be true (from P→Q) and then S from Q→S. If P is false, then R must be true (from ¬P→R) and then S from R→S. Hence the whole expression simplifies to S.
